questions.question: The 'Philips Curve' depicts the relationship between:
options: Unemployment rate and inflation rate (inverse relationship)
questions.explanation: The Phillips Curve (1958) shows an inverse relationship between unemployment and inflation — lower unemployment is associated with higher inflation and vice versa, but this relationship broke down in the 1970s stagflation.

questions.question: The law of demand states that, all else equal:
options: When price of a good rises, quantity demanded falls (inverse relationship between price and quantity demanded)
questions.explanation: Law of Demand (Alfred Marshall): price and quantity demanded have an inverse relationship — as price rises, quantity demanded falls (consumers buy less; some switch to substitutes). Exceptions (Giffen goods, Veblen goods, necessities, wartime hoarding). Demand curve slopes downward.

questions.question: The law of supply states that, all else equal:
options: When price of a good rises, quantity supplied increases (direct/positive relationship between price and quantity supplied)
Supply is constant regardless of price
questions.explanation: Law of Supply: price and quantity supplied have a direct relationship — as price rises, producers are willing to supply more (higher profit incentive). Supply curve slopes upward. Exceptions: agricultural products (time lag), antiques/art (fixed supply). Market equilibrium: demand = supply.

questions.question: The Wholesale Price Index (WPI) in India measures:
options: Price changes at the wholesale/producer level (not retail) — covers primary articles (food, minerals), fuel & power, manufactured products; base year 2011-12; used to measure producer-level inflation
questions.explanation: WPI: measures average change in prices of goods at first point of bulk sale (wholesale). Base year: 2011-12. Three groups: Primary Articles (22.6% weight — food, non-food, minerals), Fuel & Power (13.2%), Manufactured Products (64.2%). WPI has been replaced by CPI as India's headline inflation indicator (since 2014). WPI useful for PPI (Producer Price Index) analysis.

questions.question: The term 'stagflation' was coined during:
options: 1970s OPEC oil embargo crisis — when oil price quadrupled, causing supply-side cost-push inflation AND recession simultaneously; coined by UK politician Iain Macleod (1965)
questions.explanation: 'Stagflation' coined by British politician Iain Macleod (1965, combined 'stagnation' + 'inflation'). Major episode: 1973-74 OPEC oil embargo quadrupled oil prices → cost-push inflation worldwide + recession (less production) → stagflation. Challenged Keynesian consensus that inflation and unemployment are inversely related (Phillips Curve). Led to Monetarism (Friedman) gaining prominence.

questions.question: The 'Dutch Flower Auction' pricing mechanism is used for:
options: Price starting high and descending until a buyer accepts — also called 'descending auction' or 'clock auction'; used for flowers, fish, tobacco, and some treasury securities
Price starting low and going up (English auction)
questions.explanation: Dutch (Descending) Auction: auctioneer starts at high price, lowers until a buyer bids. First bidder who accepts becomes the buyer. Advantage: fast (one round). Used in: Dutch flower market (hence name), fish markets, US Treasury auctions (actually uniform price), Google's IPO. Contrasts with English (ascending) auction where price rises until one bidder remains.

questions.question: The term 'agflation' was coined during:
options: 2007-08 global food crisis — combination of agriculture + inflation; food prices rose 80%+ globally due to biofuel demand, droughts, export restrictions, and financial speculation
questions.explanation: Agflation: price surge driven by agricultural commodity prices (food inflation). 2007-08 food crisis: wheat prices rose 130%, rice 74%, corn 56%. Causes: biofuel mandates (US corn for ethanol), droughts in Australia/Ukraine, export bans (India, Vietnam, Egypt for rice), financial speculators in commodity markets. Affected food security of poor countries most.

questions.question: The Wholesale Price Index (WPI) in India has a base year of:
options: 2011-12 (revised from 2004-05 in 2017) — covers 697 commodities; primary articles 22.6%, fuel 13.2%, manufactured products 64.2%; monthly release with 14-day lag
questions.explanation: WPI base year revised to 2011-12 (released in May 2017) from 2004-05. Coverage: 697 commodities (vs 676 earlier). Weight: Primary Articles 22.62% (food: 15.26%, non-food: 4.29%, minerals: 3.07%), Fuel & Power 13.15%, Manufactured Products 64.23%. Released by Office of Economic Adviser (Ministry of Commerce). 14-day lag (faster than CPI's 12-day lag).

questions.question: India's Wholesale Price Index (WPI) inflation measures prices at:
options: First point of bulk sale/wholesale level — factory gate or farm gate prices — leads retail prices by 2-3 months (retailer passes on cost increases later)
questions.explanation: WPI: price changes at wholesale/bulk sale point — producer/factory/farm gate prices, before retail markup. WPI typically leads CPI (retail) by 2-3 months as cost increases take time to pass through supply chain. WPI doesn't include services (CPI includes services). WPI relevant for: business contracts (inflation indexation), commodity analysis.

questions.question: A Giffen good is one where demand increases as price increases because:
options: It has high brand value (Veblen effect)
It is an inferior good where the income effect dominates the substitution effect
questions.explanation: Giffen goods (Robert Giffen): inferior goods where price rise reduces real income so severely that consumers buy more (cannot afford the superior substitute). Classic example: bread in 19th-century England.

questions.question: India's Wholesale Price Index (WPI) is published by:
options: Ministry of Commerce and Industry (Office of the Economic Adviser)
questions.explanation: WPI: published monthly by Office of the Economic Adviser (OEA), Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT), Ministry of Commerce and Industry. Base year 2011-12, 697 commodities.

questions.question: India's Consumer Price Index (CPI) gives the highest weight to which category?
questions.explanation: CPI (Combined, Base 2012): Food and beverages have the highest weight ~45.86% (rural ~54.18%, urban ~36.29%). Housing: ~10.07% (urban only in combined). This high food weight makes India's CPI sensitive to food inflation.

questions.question: What is 'stagflation' and when was it first observed?
options: Simultaneous stagnation (high unemployment, low growth) + inflation (rising prices) — first observed in 1970s OPEC oil crisis, contradicting Phillips Curve assumption of inverse relationship
questions.explanation: Stagflation: simultaneous recession and inflation. Phillips Curve (1958): inverse relationship between inflation and unemployment (when inflation is high, unemployment is low). 1973 OPEC oil embargo: oil prices quadrupled → cost-push inflation PLUS economic recession → stagflation (high inflation + high unemployment simultaneously). Disproved simple Phillips Curve. Led to Monetarism (Friedman) and supply-side economics (Reagan).

questions.question: The 'Phillips Curve' in economics (A.W. Phillips, 1958) shows:
options: Inverse relationship between unemployment rate and inflation rate — when unemployment is low (economy is booming), wages/prices rise faster; when unemployment is high, inflation is low
Direct relationship between unemployment and inflation
No relationship between unemployment and inflation
Relationship between GDP and inflation only
questions.explanation: Phillips Curve (1958): negative correlation between unemployment and wage inflation in UK data 1861-1957. Policy implication: policymakers can trade-off (accept more inflation for less unemployment). Stagflation of 1970s disproved simple Phillips Curve. Long-run Phillips Curve (Friedman-Phelps): vertical at Natural Rate of Unemployment (NAIRU) — no long-run trade-off.

questions.question: The term 'Stagflation' was coined during which decade and event?
questions.explanation: Stagflation = stagnation + inflation. Coined by UK politician Iain Macleod in 1965, but the phenomenon prominently occurred in the 1970s OPEC oil crises (1973, 1979). Contradicted the Phillips Curve.

questions.question: Which of the following is the primary driver of inflation in the Indian economy?
questions.explanation: Supply-side constraints in agriculture, such as monsoon failures or procurement inefficiencies, frequently contribute to food inflation in India.

questions.question: What is the main objective of the Reserve Bank of India's open market operations?
options: A. To regulate the exchange rate
B. To control liquidity in the banking system
C. To directly finance government deficits
D. To manage public sector undertakings
questions.explanation: Open market operations involve buying/selling government securities to inject or absorb liquidity, influencing interest rates and inflation.

questions.question: How does a depreciation of the Indian rupee typically impact domestic inflation?
options: A. Reduces inflation by making exports cheaper
B. Increases inflation due to higher import costs
C. Has no direct impact on inflation
questions.explanation: A weaker rupee raises the cost of imported goods, contributing to cost-push inflation, especially in fuel and raw materials.

questions.question: In sterilized intervention by the RBI, what does the central bank do to offset the impact of foreign exchange purchases?
options: B. Sells government bonds to absorb excess liquidity
C. Reduces the cash reserve ratio
questions.explanation: Sterilization involves selling bonds to neutralize the rupee liquidity created from forex inflows, preventing inflationary pressure.

questions.question: Which fiscal indicator is most directly linked to inflation in the Indian context?
questions.explanation: A high fiscal deficit often leads to increased government spending, driving aggregate demand and inflation.

questions.question: What is the likely impact of persistent high inflation on India's balance of payments?
options: B. Worsens current account due to reduced export competitiveness
questions.explanation: High inflation makes Indian exports less competitive internationally, widening the current account deficit.

questions.question: Which agricultural reform is most likely to reduce food inflation in India?
options: A. Increase in Minimum Support Price (MSP)
B. Reduction in procurement by the government
questions.explanation: Liberalizing agricultural markets improves supply efficiency and reduces price volatility, curbing food inflation.

questions.question: Why is the Consumer Price Index (CPI) considered more relevant than the Wholesale Price Index (WPI) for inflation targeting in India?
options: B. CPI measures retail prices affecting households directly
D. CPI is easier to calculate
questions.explanation: CPI captures the price changes faced by consumers, making it more aligned with household welfare and policy objectives.

questions.question: What is the primary mechanism through which the RBI's repo rate influences inflation?
options: B. Influences borrowing costs and aggregate demand
questions.explanation: A higher repo rate reduces borrowing costs for banks, lowering aggregate demand and inflationary pressures.

questions.question: How does high inflation disproportionately affect social indicators in India?
options: B. Increases unemployment in the formal sector
C. Worsens income inequality and erodes purchasing power
questions.explanation: Inflation reduces real incomes, hits the poor harder (due to higher food weight in CPI), and exacerbates inequality.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the 'Base Effect' in the context of calculating inflation in India?
options: The impact of the previous year's price levels on the current inflation rate
The effect of base year revision on the Consumer Price Index
The influence of core inflation on the headline inflation rate
The impact of minimum support prices on food inflation
questions.explanation: Base effect refers to the distortion in current inflation rates caused by the price levels of the corresponding period in the previous year.

questions.question: If the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) decides to increase the Cash Reserve Ratio (CRR), what is the most likely immediate impact on inflation?
options: Inflation will increase due to higher costs of borrowing
Inflation will decrease as liquidity in the banking system is reduced
Inflation will remain unchanged as CRR only affects bank profitability
Inflation will increase as banks will increase lending rates to recover losses
questions.explanation: Increasing CRR reduces the loanable funds available with banks, lowering aggregate demand and helping to curb inflation.

questions.question: In the context of the Indian economy, 'Core Inflation' is calculated by excluding which of the following from the headline inflation?
questions.explanation: Core inflation excludes volatile components like food and fuel to provide a clearer picture of long-term inflation trends.

questions.question: Which of the following scenarios would lead to 'Cost-Push Inflation' in the Indian economy?
options: A sudden increase in government spending on infrastructure
A sharp rise in global crude oil prices
An increase in the money supply by the central bank
A surge in consumer demand during the festive season
questions.explanation: Cost-push inflation occurs when rising production costs, such as raw materials or oil, force producers to increase prices.

questions.question: Under the Flexible Inflation Targeting framework adopted by the RBI, what is the primary objective of the Monetary Policy Committee (MPC)?
options: To ensure price stability while keeping growth in mind
To prioritize exchange rate stability over price stability
To regulate the fiscal deficit of the central government
questions.explanation: The MPC aims to maintain price stability (targeting 4% +/- 2%) while supporting economic growth.

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of 'Stagflation'?
options: High economic growth accompanied by high inflation
Low inflation accompanied by high unemployment
High inflation accompanied by stagnant growth and high unemployment
Low inflation accompanied by stagnant growth
questions.explanation: Stagflation is a rare economic condition characterized by stagnant growth, high unemployment, and high inflation.

questions.question: What is the likely impact of 'Sterilized Intervention' by the RBI when it buys foreign currency to prevent rupee appreciation?
options: It increases the money supply and fuels inflation
It neutralizes the effect on the domestic money supply to prevent inflation
It leads to a decrease in the interest rates of government securities
It forces the government to increase the fiscal deficit
questions.explanation: Sterilization involves offsetting the increase in money supply from forex purchases by selling government securities to mop up liquidity.

questions.question: Which of the following measures is a 'Fiscal' tool to control inflation, rather than a 'Monetary' tool?
options: Selling government securities in the Open Market Operations
Increasing direct taxes to reduce disposable income
questions.explanation: Taxation is a tool of fiscal policy managed by the government, whereas repo rates and SLR are monetary tools managed by the RBI.

questions.question: When the RBI uses 'Open Market Operations' (OMO) to sell government securities, what is the intended effect on inflation?
options: To increase liquidity and stimulate growth
To decrease liquidity and put downward pressure on inflation
To stabilize the exchange rate of the rupee
To encourage commercial banks to lend more to the MSME sector
questions.explanation: Selling securities mops up excess liquidity from the system, reducing the money supply and helping to lower inflation.

questions.question: In the context of the Indian economy, which of the following is the most likely consequence of 'Cost-Push Inflation'?
options: An increase in the aggregate demand for goods and services
A decrease in the overall output and real GDP
A shift in the demand curve to the right
An increase in the purchasing power of the domestic currency
questions.explanation: Cost-push inflation occurs when production costs rise, leading to lower supply and reduced real GDP.

questions.question: Which of the following actions by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) is most effective in controlling demand-pull inflation?
options: Buying government securities through Open Market Operations
questions.explanation: Increasing CRR reduces the loanable funds available with banks, thereby curtailing aggregate demand.

questions.question: How does a significant depreciation of the Indian Rupee against the US Dollar typically impact domestic inflation?
options: It leads to deflation due to increased export competitiveness
It causes imported inflation by increasing the cost of crude oil and imports
It has no impact as India is a self-sufficient economy
It reduces inflation by increasing the supply of foreign goods
questions.explanation: Currency depreciation makes imports more expensive, which increases domestic prices, known as imported inflation.

questions.question: Which of the following statements best describes the 'Inflation Targeting' framework adopted by the RBI?
options: The RBI targets a zero-inflation rate to ensure price stability
The government sets a fixed price for all essential commodities
The RBI aims to maintain CPI inflation at 4% with a tolerance band of +/- 2%
The focus is solely on the Wholesale Price Index (WPI) to control inflation
questions.explanation: India uses a flexible inflation targeting framework based on the Consumer Price Index (CPI).

questions.question: What is the primary difference between the Wholesale Price Index (WPI) and the Consumer Price Index (CPI) in India?
options: WPI includes services, while CPI only includes goods
CPI captures the price changes at the retail level, while WPI captures them at the wholesale level
WPI is used by the RBI for inflation targeting, whereas CPI is not
CPI excludes food items, while WPI includes all food categories
questions.explanation: CPI reflects the cost of living for consumers, while WPI reflects the cost of goods for wholesalers.

questions.question: If the RBI conducts 'Sterilized Intervention' to manage the impact of capital inflows, what is the primary objective?
options: To increase the money supply to stimulate growth
To prevent the appreciation of the rupee from causing domestic deflation
To offset the effect of foreign exchange interventions on the domestic money supply
To encourage more foreign portfolio investment in the equity market
questions.explanation: Sterilization prevents the increase in domestic liquidity that occurs when the RBI buys foreign currency.

questions.question: Which of the following scenarios would lead to 'Stagflation' in an economy?
options: High inflation accompanied by high economic growth
Low inflation accompanied by high unemployment
High inflation accompanied by stagnant growth and high unemployment
Low inflation accompanied by stagnant growth
questions.explanation: Stagflation is a rare combination of stagnant economic growth, high unemployment, and high inflation.

questions.question: What is the likely impact of an increase in the 'Marginal Standing Facility (MSF)' rate on inflation?
options: It increases liquidity in the banking system, raising inflation
It makes borrowing costlier for banks, helping to curb inflation
It leads to a decrease in interest rates for retail borrowers
It encourages banks to lend more to the agricultural sector
questions.explanation: A higher MSF rate increases the cost of emergency borrowing for banks, reducing overall liquidity.

questions.question: In the context of inflation, what does the 'Base Effect' refer to?
options: The impact of the base year selection on the calculation of current inflation
The minimum interest rate set by the RBI
The effect of fiscal deficit on the price level
The impact of global oil prices on the domestic WPI
questions.explanation: The base effect occurs when the price level of the previous year affects the percentage change in current inflation.

questions.question: Which of the following is a 'fiscal' measure to control inflation, as opposed to a 'monetary' measure?
options: Selling government securities in the open market
Increasing direct taxes to reduce disposable income
questions.explanation: Taxation is a tool of fiscal policy managed by the government, whereas interest rates are monetary tools.

questions.question: In the context of the Indian economy, which of the following best describes the 'Inflation Targeting' framework adopted by the Reserve Bank of India?
options: Fixing the exchange rate of the Rupee against the US Dollar to curb imported inflation
A flexible inflation targeting framework where the RBI aims to maintain CPI inflation at 4% with a band of +/- 2%
A system where the government sets a fixed price ceiling for all essential agricultural commodities
Using only the Cash Reserve Ratio (CRR) to control the growth of broad money
questions.explanation: India uses a flexible inflation targeting framework focusing on Consumer Price Index (CPI) to maintain price stability.

questions.question: If the Reserve Bank of India decides to conduct an Open Market Operation (OMO) by selling government securities in the open market, what is the most likely intended impact on inflation?
options: Increase in liquidity, leading to higher inflation
Decrease in liquidity, helping to lower inflation
No impact on liquidity, but an increase in the exchange rate
Increase in the money supply, leading to lower interest rates
questions.explanation: Selling securities absorbs liquidity from the banking system, reducing the money supply and curbing inflationary pressure.

questions.question: Which of the following scenarios would most likely lead to 'Cost-Push Inflation' in the Indian economy?
options: A significant increase in government spending on infrastructure projects
A sudden surge in the global prices of crude oil
An increase in the disposable income of the urban middle class
A decrease in the statutory liquidity ratio (SLR) by the RBI
questions.explanation: Cost-push inflation occurs when rising production costs, like crude oil, force producers to increase prices.

questions.question: Consider the impact of Rupee depreciation on inflation. Which of the following statements is correct?
options: It reduces the cost of imported raw materials, thereby lowering inflation
It leads to 'imported inflation' by making imports more expensive
It has no impact on inflation as India is primarily an agrarian economy
It decreases the price of exports, which directly lowers domestic inflation
questions.explanation: Depreciation makes imports costlier, increasing the domestic price of goods and services, known as imported inflation.

questions.question: Which of the following is the primary difference between the Wholesale Price Index (WPI) and the Consumer Price Index (CPI) in India?
options: WPI includes services, while CPI only includes goods
CPI includes services and reflects the retail price, while WPI tracks goods at the wholesale level
WPI is used by the RBI for inflation targeting, whereas CPI is used by the Ministry of Finance
CPI tracks price changes at the factory gate, while WPI tracks the end-consumer price
questions.explanation: CPI captures the price of a basket of goods and services consumed by households, unlike WPI which focuses on wholesale goods.

questions.question: What is the most likely effect of an increase in the Repo Rate by the RBI on the inflation rate?
options: Borrowing becomes cheaper, increasing consumption and inflation
Borrowing becomes costlier, reducing aggregate demand and lowering inflation
It leads to an immediate increase in the money supply in the economy
It encourages banks to lower their lending rates to stimulate growth
questions.explanation: Higher repo rates increase borrowing costs for banks and consumers, reducing spending and cooling down inflation.

questions.question: In the context of monetary policy, what does 'Sterilization' refer to when the RBI intervenes in the foreign exchange market?
options: Removing all gold reserves from the banking system to control inflation
Neutralizing the effect of foreign exchange inflows on the domestic money supply using OMOs
Fixing the exchange rate to prevent the depreciation of the Rupee
Increasing the CRR to prevent banks from lending to the export sector
questions.explanation: Sterilization prevents excess liquidity from foreign exchange interventions from causing inflation by selling government bonds.

questions.question: How does an increase in the Cash Reserve Ratio (CRR) help in controlling inflation?
options: It increases the amount of loanable funds available with commercial banks
It reduces the credit creation capacity of banks, reducing the money supply
It encourages banks to invest more in government securities
It lowers the cost of deposits for the general public
questions.explanation: Higher CRR forces banks to keep more cash with the RBI, reducing the funds available for lending and curbing inflation.

questions.question: Which of the following is a 'Demand-Pull' factor contributing to inflation?
options: An increase in the minimum support price (MSP) for crops
A sharp increase in the price of imported electronic components
Excessive printing of currency notes by the central bank
A disruption in the supply chain due to a natural calamity
questions.explanation: Excess money supply increases aggregate demand, pulling prices up when supply cannot keep pace.

questions.question: Which of the following scenarios is most likely to lead to 'Cost-Push Inflation' in the Indian economy?
options: A sudden increase in government spending on infrastructure
A sharp rise in global crude oil prices
An increase in the disposable income of the urban middle class
A decrease in the Cash Reserve Ratio (CRR) by the RBI
questions.explanation: Cost-push inflation occurs when production costs rise, such as higher fuel prices, forcing firms to increase prices.

questions.question: If the Reserve Bank of India (RBI) decides to adopt a 'Hawkish' monetary policy stance, which of the following actions is it most likely to take?
options: B. Purchase government securities from the open market
C. Increase the Statutory Liquidity Ratio (SLR)
D. Lower the Reverse Repo Rate
questions.explanation: A hawkish stance aims to curb inflation by tightening money supply, often through increasing policy rates or reserve requirements.

questions.question: Consider the following statements regarding 'Headline Inflation' and 'Core Inflation'. Which of the following is correct?
options: A. Core inflation includes food and fuel prices to show total inflation
B. Headline inflation excludes volatile components like food and energy
C. Core inflation is a better indicator of long-term inflation trends
D. Headline inflation is always lower than core inflation
questions.explanation: Core inflation excludes volatile food and fuel prices, providing a clearer view of the underlying long-term inflation trend.

questions.question: How does a depreciation of the Indian Rupee against the US Dollar typically impact domestic inflation?
options: A. It leads to deflation due to increased exports
B. It causes 'imported inflation' by increasing the cost of imports
C. It has no impact as inflation is purely a domestic phenomenon
D. It decreases inflation by making domestic goods cheaper
questions.explanation: Depreciation makes imports like crude oil more expensive, which increases domestic production costs and prices.

questions.question: In the context of the Monetary Policy Committee (MPC), what is the primary objective of the 'Inflation Targeting' framework adopted by India?
options: A. To maintain a zero-inflation environment
B. To prioritize GDP growth over price stability
C. To maintain price stability while keeping in mind the objective of growth
D. To fix the exchange rate of the Rupee against the Dollar
questions.explanation: India's flexible inflation targeting framework aims for price stability (4% +/- 2%) while supporting economic growth.

questions.question: What is the impact of 'Sterilization' conducted by the RBI during a period of heavy capital inflows?
options: A. It increases the money supply to stimulate growth
B. It neutralizes the effect of foreign exchange inflows on the domestic money supply
C. It leads to a rapid depreciation of the domestic currency
D. It forces the government to increase fiscal deficits
questions.explanation: Sterilization involves selling government bonds to mop up excess liquidity created by buying foreign currency.

questions.question: Which of the following indicators is used as the primary measure for the RBI's inflation targeting in India?
questions.explanation: The RBI uses the CPI (Combined) as its primary anchor for inflation targeting as it reflects retail price changes.

questions.question: What happens to the 'Real Interest Rate' if the nominal interest rate remains constant while inflation increases?
options: A. The real interest rate increases
B. The real interest rate decreases
C. The real interest rate remains unchanged
D. The nominal interest rate automatically adjusts to compensate
questions.explanation: Real Interest Rate = Nominal Rate - Inflation; therefore, rising inflation reduces the real return on investment.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of 'Demand-Pull Inflation'?
options: A. An increase in the minimum support price (MSP) for crops
B. A sudden increase in consumer spending due to a tax cut
C. A disruption in the supply chain due to a pandemic
D. An increase in the corporate tax rate
questions.explanation: Demand-pull inflation occurs when aggregate demand exceeds aggregate supply, often driven by increased spending.

questions.question: Which of the following monetary policy tools directly influences the cost of borrowing for commercial banks in India?
questions.explanation: Bank Rate is the rate at which the RBI lends to banks, affecting their borrowing cost.

questions.question: A persistent rise in food prices in India is most likely to cause which of the following immediate effects on the Consumer Price Index (CPI)?
questions.explanation: Food items have a high weight in CPI, so rising food prices lift the overall index.

questions.question: If the Indian rupee depreciates sharply against the US dollar, which component of the balance of payments is most directly affected?
questions.explanation: Depreciation makes imports costlier and exports cheaper, widening the trade deficit.

questions.question: Sterilized foreign exchange intervention by the RBI aims to:
options: Increase money supply to boost growth
Neutralize the impact of FX intervention on domestic liquidity
Peg the rupee to a basket of currencies
questions.explanation: Sterilisation offsets liquidity changes caused by buying/selling foreign exchange.

questions.question: Which fiscal indicator is most closely watched to gauge demand‑pull inflationary pressure in the Indian economy?
options: Capital expenditure as % of total expenditure
questions.explanation: A high fiscal deficit can boost aggregate demand, leading to demand‑pull inflation.

questions.question: The introduction of the 'e-NAM' platform primarily aims to reduce inflation in agricultural commodities by:
options: Improving price discovery and market integration
questions.explanation: e‑NAM connects markets, enhancing price discovery and reducing price volatility.

questions.question: Which of the following would most likely lead to cost‑push inflation in India?
options: Increase in GST on luxury goods
questions.explanation: Higher oil prices raise production costs, passing through to consumer prices.

questions.question: Under the RBI's Monetary Policy Committee, a decision to raise the repo rate primarily signals:
options: A move to increase fiscal deficit
A strategy to depreciate the rupee
questions.explanation: Higher repo rates make credit expensive, cooling demand and inflation.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the relationship between the Wholesale Price Index (WPI) and Consumer Price Index (CPI) in India?
options: WPI always leads CPI by one quarter
CPI includes services while WPI is limited to goods
CPI is a subset of WPI
Both indices use identical basket weights
questions.explanation: CPI covers both goods and services; WPI mainly tracks wholesale prices of goods.

questions.question: If the government reduces subsidies on diesel, the immediate impact on inflation is expected to be:
options: Supply‑side inflation due to higher transport costs
Demand‑side inflation from increased consumer spending
questions.explanation: Higher diesel prices raise logistics costs, feeding into overall price levels.

questions.question: In the context of the Indian economy, which of the following statements best describes the 'Inflation Targeting' framework adopted by the Reserve Bank of India?
options: The RBI aims to maintain a zero-inflation rate to ensure price stability.
The RBI targets a specific inflation range, with a primary focus on the Wholesale Price Index (WPI).
The RBI targets a flexible inflation target based on the Consumer Price Index (CPI) combined.
The target is set unilaterally by the RBI without consultation with the Government of India.
questions.explanation: India uses a flexible inflation targeting framework focusing on CPI (Combined) with a target of 4% (+/- 2%).

questions.question: If the Reserve Bank of India decides to increase the Marginal Standing Facility (MSF) rate, what is the most probable immediate impact on inflation?
options: Inflation will rise due to increased liquidity in the banking system.
Inflation will likely decrease as borrowing costs for banks increase, reducing money supply.
It will have no impact on inflation as MSF is a last-resort window.
It will lead to immediate rupee appreciation, causing demand-pull inflation.
questions.explanation: Higher MSF rates make borrowing more expensive for banks, tightening liquidity and helping curb inflation.

questions.question: Consider the impact of 'Rupee Depreciation' on inflation. Which of the following is a correct consequence?
options: It leads to 'imported inflation' due to the rising cost of essential imports like crude oil.
It reduces the cost of imported capital goods, thereby lowering domestic inflation.
It leads to a decrease in the prices of exported goods in the international market.
It automatically reduces the domestic inflation rate by boosting exports.
questions.explanation: A weaker rupee makes imports more expensive, increasing the cost of inputs and causing imported inflation.

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of 'Stagflation' in an economy?
options: High economic growth accompanied by high inflation.
Low inflation accompanied by high unemployment and low growth.
High inflation accompanied by stagnant economic growth and high unemployment.
Rapidly falling prices accompanied by high industrial production.
questions.explanation: Stagflation is a rare combination of stagnant economic growth, high unemployment, and high inflation.

questions.question: How does 'Sterilized Intervention' by the RBI typically affect the domestic money supply during periods of high capital inflows?
options: It increases the money supply to prevent the rupee from appreciating.
It offsets the increase in money supply caused by foreign exchange purchases by selling government securities.
It encourages capital flight to maintain the balance of payments equilibrium.
It lowers the interest rates to discourage foreign investment.
questions.explanation: Sterilization prevents excess liquidity from entering the economy by absorbing funds through Open Market Operations.

questions.question: Which of the following measures is a 'Fiscal' rather than a 'Monetary' tool to control inflation?
options: Reducing government expenditure and increasing direct taxes.
Selling government bonds in the Open Market Operations.
questions.explanation: Fiscal tools involve government spending and taxation, whereas Repo and SLR are monetary tools used by the RBI.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between 'Headline Inflation' and 'Core Inflation'?
options: Headline inflation excludes services, while core inflation includes them.
Headline inflation includes all items, while core inflation excludes volatile food and fuel prices.
Core inflation is measured by WPI, while headline inflation is measured by CPI.
Headline inflation is a long-term trend, while core inflation is a short-term fluctuation.
questions.explanation: Core inflation strips out volatile components like food and energy to show the underlying inflation trend.

questions.question: In the context of 'Demand-Pull Inflation', which of the following would act as a catalyst?
options: A sharp increase in the corporate tax rate.
A sudden increase in the minimum support price (MSP) for crops.
An increase in the money supply through aggressive quantitative easing.
A decrease in the demand for luxury goods.
questions.explanation: Increased money supply boosts aggregate demand, pulling prices up when supply cannot keep pace.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the 'Phillips Curve' relationship in the short run?
options: A direct relationship between inflation and unemployment.
An inverse relationship between inflation and unemployment.
A linear relationship between interest rates and inflation.
No relationship between inflation and economic growth.
questions.explanation: The Phillips Curve suggests that as unemployment decreases, inflation tends to rise in the short run.

questions.question: Which of the following monetary policy actions is most likely to curb demand‑pull inflation in India?
options: Open market purchase of government securities
Reduction in the cash reserve ratio
Sterilised foreign exchange intervention to curb rupee appreciation
questions.explanation: Raising the repo rate makes borrowing costlier, reducing demand pressure.

questions.question: A sharp depreciation of the Indian rupee against the US dollar is expected to:
questions.explanation: Weaker rupee raises the cost of imported goods, feeding price rises.

questions.question: Which component of the Balance of Payments directly reflects the impact of inflation differentials between India and its trading partners?
questions.explanation: The current account records trade in goods and services, affected by relative price changes.

questions.question: If the government raises indirect taxes on petroleum products, the immediate effect on inflation is:
options: Cost‑push inflation through higher transport costs
Demand‑pull inflation due to higher consumer spending
questions.explanation: Higher fuel taxes raise production costs, leading to cost‑push inflation.

questions.question: Sterilised intervention by the RBI to mop up excess liquidity after a large inflow of foreign capital primarily aims to:
questions.explanation: Absorbing liquidity curtails money supply growth, limiting demand‑pull inflation.

questions.question: Which of the following fiscal indicators, if rising sharply, is most likely to fuel inflationary pressure?
questions.explanation: A higher primary deficit indicates greater borrowing and spending, adding to demand pressure.

questions.question: The introduction of the PM‑Kisan scheme, which provides direct cash transfers to farmers, is expected to influence inflation by:
options: Reducing food price inflation through higher supply
Increasing demand‑pull inflation in rural markets
Lowering overall CPI through wage stability
Neutral effect as it is a supply‑side measure
questions.explanation: Extra cash raises rural demand, potentially pushing up food prices.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the relationship between the GDP deflator and CPI in the context of Indian inflation?
options: GDP deflator includes only goods, CPI includes goods and services
CPI reflects household consumption basket, GDP deflator covers all final goods and services produced domestically
Both measure exactly the same price changes
GDP deflator is a leading indicator of CPI movements
questions.explanation: CPI is consumption‑focused; the GDP deflator captures price changes for all domestically produced final output.

questions.question: A persistent rise in food price inflation in India is most directly linked to:
questions.explanation: Food prices are highly sensitive to agricultural supply disruptions.

questions.question: When the RBI conducts a repo operation at a higher rate than the market rate, the expected impact on inflation expectations is:
options: Inflation expectations rise due to tighter liquidity
Inflation expectations fall as higher rates signal anti‑inflation stance
Inflation expectations become volatile irrespective of rate level
questions.explanation: Higher repo rates signal a commitment to contain inflation, lowering expectations.

questions.question: Which monetary policy tool directly reduces the money supply to curb inflation without affecting the exchange rate?
options: Open market sale of government securities
questions.explanation: Selling securities withdraws liquidity from banks, tightening money supply while leaving exchange rate largely unaffected.

questions.question: A sharp depreciation of the rupee most likely leads to which type of inflation in India?
questions.explanation: A weaker rupee raises import costs, translating into higher consumer price levels—imported‑price inflation.

questions.question: How does a sterilised intervention affect the domestic price level when the RBI sells foreign exchange reserves?
options: It increases the money supply and raises inflation
It decreases the money supply and reduces inflation
It has no effect on money supply but reduces inflation
It increases the money supply but reduces inflation
questions.explanation: Selling foreign reserves absorbs domestic currency, contracting money supply and dampening inflationary pressures.

questions.question: Which fiscal indicator is most indicative of a fiscal deficit’s potential to fuel inflation?
questions.explanation: A larger revenue deficit usually implies higher borrowing, increasing money supply and inflationary risk.

questions.question: What is the likely impact on inflation when the government implements large‑scale agricultural procurement schemes during a drought?
options: Inflation rises due to higher demand
Inflation falls due to price stabilization
questions.explanation: Procurement stabilises prices by ensuring supply, thereby curbing price rises.

questions.question: Which trade balance component directly reflects the effect of higher import prices on domestic inflation?
questions.explanation: A widening trade deficit due to higher import costs feeds into consumer prices, raising inflation.

questions.question: Which of the following policies is least likely to help control core inflation in India?
options: Increase in the statutory Liquidity Coverage Ratio
Reduction in the Cash Reserve Ratio for banks
questions.explanation: Lowering CRR injects liquidity, potentially stoking inflation rather than curbing it.

questions.question: The concept of 'infrastructure-driven inflation' in India primarily refers to inflation caused by:
options: Rapid expansion of transport and energy networks
High import tariffs on capital goods
questions.explanation: Large infrastructure spending raises demand for raw materials, pushing up prices.

questions.question: During a period of high inflation, a central bank is likely to adopt which of the following interventions?
options: Reduce the tax on consumer goods
questions.explanation: Higher SLR forces banks to hold more reserves, tightening money supply and easing inflation.

questions.question: Which social indicator can serve as a proxy for inflationary pressures on the poor in India?
questions.explanation: Food inflation directly affects the cost of living for the poor, reflecting inflationary distress.

questions.question: Which of the following is a primary tool used by the RBI to curb high inflation in India?
questions.explanation: Rising repo rate raises borrowing costs, reducing money supply and inflationary pressure.

questions.question: How does a depreciation of the rupee typically affect food price inflation in India?
options: It tends to increase food price inflation
It has no significant effect on food prices
questions.explanation: A weaker rupee raises import costs, pushing up the price of food items that rely on imported inputs.

questions.question: Which of the following is a consequence of the RBI’s sterilised intervention in foreign exchange markets?
options: It temporarily reduces domestic money supply
It directly lowers the price of agricultural commodities
It eliminates the need for monetary policy
questions.explanation: Sterilised intervention sells foreign exchange and buys domestic currency, absorbing excess liquidity.

questions.question: Which fiscal indicator is most closely watched as a precursor to rising inflation in India?
questions.explanation: A widening current account deficit signals higher imports, which can feed inflation.

questions.question: What is the likely impact on inflation when the Reserve Bank of India cuts the Cash Reserve Ratio (CRR)?
questions.explanation: Lower CRR releases liquidity to banks, potentially boosting spending and inflation.

questions.question: Which sector’s reforms are expected to have the most direct effect on reducing food inflation in India?
questions.explanation: Improved agri‑transport reduces post‑harvest losses, stabilising food prices.

questions.question: A rise in the consumer price index (CPI) in India is most likely to be caused by which of the following?
options: Increase in the real wage index
Growth in domestic production of staples
Increase in fuel and food price indexes
questions.explanation: CPI rises when the components such as fuel and food prices climb.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the relationship between monetary policy and the balance of payments in India?
options: Tightening monetary policy usually improves the current account balance
Expansionary policy always worsens the current account
Monetary policy has no effect on the balance of payments
Only fiscal policy affects the balance of payments
questions.explanation: Higher rates attract capital, strengthening the rupee and improving the current account.

questions.question: Which of the following is a likely consequence of sustained low inflation in India?
questions.explanation: Low inflation boosts real wages as nominal wages rise slower than prices.

questions.question: What role does the Wholesale Price Index (WPI) play in India’s inflation targeting framework?
options: It is the sole indicator for setting the repo rate
It provides a lagging measure of inflation for policy assessment
It replaces CPI in all policy decisions
It has no relevance to monetary policy
questions.explanation: WPI, being a lagging indicator, helps assess inflation trends but CPI is primary for policy decisions.

questions.question: A sustained depreciation of the Indian rupee against the US dollar is most likely to:
questions.explanation: Depreciation makes imports costlier, widening the current account deficit.

questions.question: Sterilised foreign exchange intervention by the RBI aims to:
options: Increase money supply to boost inflation
Neutralise the impact of FX market operations on liquidity
questions.explanation: Sterilisation offsets liquidity changes caused by FX interventions.

questions.question: Which fiscal indicator is most closely associated with demand‑pull inflation?
options: Fiscal deficit as a % of GDP
Revenue deficit as a % of GDP
Primary deficit as a % of GDP
Capital expenditure as a % of GDP
questions.explanation: A high fiscal deficit injects excess demand, fueling demand‑pull inflation.

questions.question: In the context of agricultural reforms, the removal of minimum support price (MSP) controls would most likely:
options: Reduce price volatility and long‑run inflation
questions.explanation: Market‑driven pricing reduces price distortions and long‑run inflation.

questions.question: Which component of the balance of payments directly reflects the impact of oil price shocks on Indian inflation?
questions.explanation: Oil imports are part of trade in goods, affecting the current account and inflation.

questions.question: If the RBI raises the policy repo rate, the immediate effect on the CPI inflation rate is expected to be:
questions.explanation: Higher repo rates curb credit growth, leading to a slower rise in CPI.

questions.question: Which of the following social indicators is most likely to rise during a period of high food inflation?
questions.explanation: Food inflation erodes real incomes, pushing more households below the poverty line.

questions.question: The term 'cost‑push inflation' in the Indian context is most commonly triggered by:
options: Wage hikes due to strong labour unions
questions.explanation: Higher global commodity prices raise input costs, leading to cost‑push inflation.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the relationship between trade balance and inflation is correct?
options: A trade surplus always leads to higher inflation
A widening trade deficit exerts upward pressure on domestic price levels
Trade balance has no impact on inflation
A trade surplus reduces the need for monetary tightening
questions.explanation: A larger deficit means more expensive imports, pushing up prices.

questions.question: A persistent rise in food prices in India is most likely to cause:
questions.explanation: Food items have a large weight in CPI, so their price rise lifts headline inflation.

questions.question: If the Indian rupee depreciates against the US dollar, the immediate impact on the current account is likely to be:
questions.explanation: Depreciation makes imports costlier and exports cheaper, improving the trade balance.

questions.question: Sterilised foreign exchange intervention by RBI aims to:
questions.explanation: Sterilisation offsets the liquidity effect of FX market operations.

questions.question: Which fiscal indicator directly reflects the government's ability to control demand‑pull inflation?
questions.explanation: A lower primary deficit reduces aggregate demand pressure, helping contain demand‑pull inflation.

questions.question: The 'inflation tax' on holders of cash primarily arises because:
options: Higher interest rates reduce cash demand
Money loses purchasing power over time
questions.explanation: Inflation erodes the real value of cash balances, acting like a tax.

questions.question: In the context of agricultural reforms, a shift from minimum support price (MSP) to market‑linked pricing is expected to:
options: Make food prices more responsive to supply‑demand
questions.explanation: Market‑linked pricing aligns farmgate prices with actual market conditions, affecting inflation dynamics.

questions.question: Which component of the Balance of Payments is most directly affected by changes in the inflation differential between India and its trading partners?
questions.explanation: Inflation differentials influence export‑import price competitiveness, altering the trade balance.

questions.question: A rise in the Wholesale Price Index (WPI) without a corresponding rise in Consumer Price Index (CPI) suggests:
options: Weak transmission of producer price changes
questions.explanation: If WPI rises but CPI does not, producer price changes are not fully passed to consumers.

questions.question: Which social indicator is most likely to worsen during a period of high inflation?
questions.explanation: High inflation erodes real wages, reducing purchasing power of households.

questions.question: If the RBI conducts a sterilised intervention by selling foreign exchange reserves, the primary macro‑economic effect is to:
options: Raise the exchange rate without changing money supply
Lower the exchange rate while expanding money supply
questions.explanation: Selling foreign assets absorbs rupees, offsetting liquidity created by the FX purchase.

questions.question: Which fiscal indicator directly reflects the government's ability to finance a fiscal deficit without creating inflationary pressure?
options: Primary fiscal deficit as a % of GDP
Revenue deficit as a % of GDP
Fiscal deficit financed through market borrowing
questions.explanation: A primary deficit excludes interest costs and shows current spending gap; if low, inflation risk is limited.

questions.question: The introduction of the PM‑Kisan Scheme is expected to influence inflation primarily through:
options: Direct price control on food grains
questions.explanation: Cash transfers to farmers aim to boost production, easing food‑price inflation.

questions.question: Which component of the Balance of Payments is most directly affected when India experiences imported inflation due to higher oil prices?
options: Official reserves – changes in RBI holdings
questions.explanation: Higher oil import bills widen the trade deficit, a key current‑account item.

questions.question: Cost‑push inflation in India can arise when:
options: Real wages rise faster than productivity
questions.explanation: Higher real wages raise production costs, which firms pass on as higher prices.

questions.question: Which of the following social indicators is most likely to deteriorate first during a period of high inflation?
questions.explanation: Inflation erodes real incomes, pushing more households below the poverty line.

questions.question: A rise in the WPI (Wholesale Price Index) relative to the CPI (Consumer Price Index) suggests:
options: Future consumer inflation is likely to fall
questions.explanation: Higher wholesale prices indicate cost pressures at the production stage.

questions.question: When the government raises indirect taxes on fuel, the immediate effect on inflation is:
options: Raise headline inflation through higher transport costs
Have no effect on inflation as taxes are fiscal
questions.explanation: Higher fuel taxes raise transport and logistics costs, feeding into overall price levels.

questions.question: Which of the following actions by the RBI is most likely to curb demand‑pull inflation without affecting the exchange rate?
options: Open market sale of government securities
Increase in the Cash Reserve Ratio
questions.explanation: Open market sales withdraw liquidity, reducing demand pressure while leaving FX operations untouched.

questions.question: A sharp depreciation of the rupee can lead to higher CPI inflation primarily because:
options: It increases the price of imported oil and commodities
It forces the RBI to cut policy rates
questions.explanation: A weaker rupee makes imports costlier, especially oil, feeding into consumer price indices.

questions.question: If the government raises its fiscal deficit while the current account deficit remains unchanged, the likely impact on inflation is:
options: Deflationary pressure due to higher savings
Higher inflation due to increased aggregate demand
No effect as fiscal and external sectors are unrelated
Lower inflation because of a stronger rupee
questions.explanation: Higher fiscal deficit raises domestic demand, adding to inflationary pressure.

questions.question: Which component of the Balance of Payments directly reflects the impact of food price inflation on the Indian economy?
questions.explanation: Food price changes affect the export‑import of agricultural commodities, recorded under trade in goods.

questions.question: In the context of agricultural reforms, removal of minimum support price (MSP) controls is most likely to:
options: Increase rural inflation by raising food prices
Reduce food price volatility and hence lower CPI inflation
questions.explanation: Market‑driven pricing can smooth price spikes, reducing food‑inflation volatility.

questions.question: Which monetary policy tool is specifically used to neutralise the inflationary impact of an inflow of foreign exchange?
questions.explanation: Sterilised intervention offsets liquidity created by foreign exchange inflows, curbing inflation.

questions.question: Higher real wages without a corresponding rise in productivity tend to:
questions.explanation: Higher real wages raise production costs, feeding into cost‑push inflation.

questions.question: If the RBI reduces the policy repo rate while the government simultaneously expands its capital expenditure, the net effect on headline inflation is likely to be:
options: Neutral, as the two actions offset each other
Uncertain, depends on exchange rate movements
questions.explanation: Both lower rates and higher spending boost demand, reinforcing inflationary pressure.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why a rise in global commodity prices can cause imported inflation in India even if the rupee remains stable?
options: Global price hikes raise the FOB price paid for imports
The RBI must increase the repo rate
questions.explanation: FOB (Free on Board) prices rise globally, raising import costs irrespective of exchange rates.

questions.question: The 'inflation tax' on holders of cash balances arises because:
options: The government levies a direct tax on cash
Higher inflation erodes the real value of nominal cash holdings
Cash deposits attract higher interest rates
The RBI imposes a liquidity surcharge
questions.explanation: Inflation reduces purchasing power of cash, acting like an implicit tax on holders.

questions.question: If the Indian rupee depreciates sharply against the US dollar, the immediate impact on the Consumer Price Index (CPI) is most likely to be:
options: An increase due to higher import‑priced goods
A decrease because exports become cheaper
No change as CPI excludes imported items
A fall in food prices only
questions.explanation: Depreciation raises the domestic price of imported goods, which are part of the CPI basket.

questions.question: Sterilised intervention by the RBI to offset the inflationary impact of capital inflows involves:
options: Selling government bonds to absorb excess liquidity
Buying foreign currency to increase reserves
questions.explanation: Selling bonds mops up the rupee excess liquidity created by foreign inflows, limiting inflationary pressure.

questions.question: Which fiscal indicator directly reflects the government's ability to finance a fiscal deficit without adding to inflationary pressure?
questions.explanation: A primary surplus means non‑interest spending is below revenue, allowing deficit financing without extra borrowing that fuels inflation.

questions.question: Under the recent agricultural reforms, the shift to market‑linked procurement is expected to influence inflation by:
options: Improving price transmission and reducing food price volatility
Fixing farm prices and eliminating all price fluctuations
Increasing government buffer stocks to control prices
Mandating minimum support prices for all crops
questions.explanation: Market‑linked procurement enhances price discovery and can dampen abrupt food price spikes.

questions.question: A persistent current account deficit in India can contribute to inflation mainly because:
options: It puts downward pressure on the rupee, raising import‑priced inflation
It leads to higher foreign exchange reserves, which are inflationary
It forces the RBI to raise the repo rate automatically
It increases domestic savings, reducing demand‑pull pressures
questions.explanation: A current account deficit often weakens the rupee, making imports costlier and feeding into CPI inflation.

questions.question: The Reserve Bank of India conducts a sterilized intervention by selling foreign exchange reserves in the market. What is the most likely immediate impact of this action on inflation in the Indian economy?
options: A. It increases money supply, thereby raising inflation.
B. It reduces money supply, thereby putting downward pressure on inflation.
C. It has no effect on money supply but strengthens the rupee, which directly raises inflation.
D. It raises fiscal deficit, leading to higher inflation.
questions.explanation: Selling reserves withdraws liquidity, shrinking the money supply and dampening inflationary pressure.

questions.question: If the RBI conducts a sterilised intervention by selling government bonds after a large inflow of foreign capital, the immediate effect on the domestic money supply and inflation is:
options: Money supply unchanged; inflation pressure reduced
Money supply increased; inflation pressure heightened
Money supply unchanged; inflation pressure unchanged
Money supply decreased; inflation pressure reduced
questions.explanation: Sterilisation offsets the liquidity impact of the capital inflow, leaving money supply unchanged and curbing inflationary pressure.

questions.question: A sharp depreciation of the rupee is likely to raise food inflation in India primarily because:
options: Higher import duties on agricultural inputs
Increased cost of imported fertilizers and oilseeds
Rise in domestic wages due to export boom
questions.explanation: Depreciation makes imported agricultural inputs like fertilizers costlier, feeding into higher food prices.

questions.question: Which of the following fiscal measures would most directly help contain demand‑pull inflation without worsening the fiscal deficit?
options: Increasing GST rates on luxury goods
Introducing a targeted cash transfer to low‑income households
questions.explanation: Higher GST on luxury items reduces demand for non‑essential goods, tempering inflation with minimal impact on the deficit.

questions.question: During 2023‑24, India's current account surplus narrowed despite a rise in export volumes. The most plausible explanation is:
options: A surge in remittances offsetting export gains
Higher import bill for oil and gold outweighing export earnings
Sharp fall in foreign direct investment inflows
Depreciation of the rupee making imports cheaper
questions.explanation: Rising import costs, especially oil and gold, can erode a current‑account surplus even when exports grow.

questions.question: The introduction of the e-NAM platform is expected to affect food inflation by:
options: Increasing price volatility through electronic trading
Reducing middle‑men margins and improving price transmission
Mandating minimum support prices above market rates
Restricting farmer access to national markets
questions.explanation: e‑NAM links markets, lowering intermediaries' cut and enhancing price pass‑through, which can temper food inflation.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a transmission channel through which monetary policy influences inflation in India?
questions.explanation: Fiscal deficit is a fiscal, not monetary, transmission mechanism.

questions.question: If the RBI raises the repo rate while simultaneously the government expands its fiscal deficit, the net effect on headline inflation is likely to be:
options: Neutral, as monetary tightening offsets fiscal expansion
Uncertain, depending on the magnitude of each policy
Deflationary, due to higher interest rates dominating
questions.explanation: The opposing forces create ambiguity; the dominant effect depends on relative policy sizes.

questions.question: Which component of the Wholesale Price Index (WPI) is most sensitive to changes in global oil prices, thereby influencing overall inflation forecasts?
questions.explanation: Fuel & power includes petroleum products directly linked to global oil price movements.

questions.question: A rise in the index of agricultural wages without a commensurate rise in productivity primarily leads to:
questions.explanation: Higher wage costs without productivity gains raise production costs, feeding cost‑push food inflation.

questions.question: During a period of high inflation, the government decides to raise the GSDP growth target but simultaneously tightens import duties on non‑essential items. The likely impact on the trade‑off between growth and inflation is:
questions.explanation: Higher growth targets boost demand, while higher duties increase prices, together pushing inflation up.

questions.question: If the RBI decides to conduct a sterilized intervention by selling government securities after a large inflow of foreign exchange, the immediate effect on the money supply and the exchange rate is:
questions.explanation: Sterilisation offsets the liquidity impact, keeping money supply unchanged while the direct purchase of foreign currency supports rupee appreciation.

questions.question: A persistent rise in food and fuel prices pushes the CPI inflation above the RBI's 4% target. Which of the following fiscal measures would most directly help contain headline inflation without curbing aggregate demand markedly?
options: Increasing excise duty on petroleum products
Reducing subsidies on LPG for households
Targeted cash transfers to BPL families
Expanding the Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Scheme
questions.explanation: Cutting subsidies raises fuel prices, directly tempering food‑fuel inflation, while other options either boost demand or are demand‑neutral.

questions.question: During a period of high import‑dependent inflation, the current account shows a widening deficit while the capital account records a surplus. Which macro‑economic implication is most likely?
options: Exchange rate depreciation will be limited by capital inflows
Monetary tightening will automatically correct the current‑account deficit
Fiscal expansion is necessary to sustain growth
RBI must sell foreign exchange reserves to prevent rupee appreciation
questions.explanation: Capital inflows offset the pressure of a current‑account deficit, restraining rupee depreciation despite import‑price inflation.

questions.question: The government introduces a price‑linked agricultural procurement policy that guarantees MSPs above market rates. Which of the following is a likely short‑term impact on inflation?
options: Core inflation will fall due to higher farmer incomes
Food‑price inflation will rise as procurement costs are passed on
Overall CPI will remain unchanged because procurement is limited to cereals
questions.explanation: Higher MSPs increase procurement costs, which can be transmitted to retail food prices, raising food‑price inflation.

questions.question: If the RBI lowers the repo rate while simultaneously increasing the CRR, the net effect on liquidity and inflation is:
options: Liquidity rises, but inflationary pressure may not change much
questions.explanation: A lower repo rate adds liquidity, but a higher CRR withdraws it; the offsetting actions leave liquidity ambiguous, so inflation impact is muted.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the 'cost‑push' component of Indian inflation during a global oil price shock?
options: Higher wages demanded by organized labour
Rising import duty on crude oil increasing production costs
Increased money supply due to RBI's open market operations
Higher consumer demand for luxury goods
questions.explanation: Higher import duties raise oil import costs, which feed into production costs and constitute cost‑push inflation.

questions.question: During a phase of moderate inflation, the fiscal deficit as a percentage of GDP is 6.5% and the primary deficit is 2.5%. What does this indicate about the role of debt servicing in the fiscal stance?
options: Debt servicing accounts for roughly 4% of GDP
Primary deficit is larger than total deficit
questions.explanation: Total deficit minus primary deficit equals debt‑service cost (~4% of GDP).

questions.question: If the RBI adopts a tight monetary stance while the government simultaneously expands indirect tax revenue, the likely combined effect on CPI inflation is:
options: Accelerated rise in CPI due to fiscal expansion
Neutral impact as monetary tightening offsets fiscal stimulus
Sharp decline in CPI because both policies are contractionary
No effect because CPI is driven only by supply shocks
questions.explanation: Both tighter money and higher tax collections reduce demand, leading to downward pressure on CPI.

questions.question: A persistent rise in the real effective exchange rate (REER) of the Indian rupee tends to:
options: Boost export‑led growth and lower inflation
Worsen trade balance and increase imported inflation
Reduce imported inflation while improving current account
Have no impact on domestic price levels
questions.explanation: An appreciated REER makes imports cheaper but hurts export competitiveness, widening trade deficit and raising imported‑inflation component.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the relationship between wage inflation and CPI inflation in India is most accurate?
options: Wage inflation always leads CPI inflation with a one‑year lag
CPI inflation drives wage negotiations, not the reverse
Both can reinforce each other, but wage growth is more pronounced in the organized sector
Wage inflation is irrelevant for CPI because food prices dominate the index
questions.explanation: Wage and price inflation can be mutually reinforcing, especially where organized‑sector wages form a sizable share of consumption.

questions.question: If the RBI conducts a sterilised intervention by selling government securities after buying foreign exchange, the immediate effect on the money supply is:
questions.explanation: Sterilisation offsets the liquidity impact of FX purchase, leaving money supply unchanged.

questions.question: A persistent rise in food prices is causing headline inflation to stay above 6% while core inflation remains at 4%. Which of the following policy responses is most appropriate for the RBI?
options: Tighten liquidity only through open market operations
Maintain policy rate and focus on supply‑side measures
questions.explanation: Core inflation is within target; tightening may hurt growth, so focus on supply‑side while keeping rates unchanged.

questions.question: Depreciation of the Indian rupee against the US dollar tends to affect inflation through which of the following channels?
options: Both import‑price pass‑through and export‑price indexation
questions.explanation: A weaker rupee raises import prices and can raise export prices, both feeding into overall inflation.

questions.question: During a fiscal consolidation, the government reduces its expenditure on agricultural subsidies. The likely short‑run impact on food inflation is:
options: No impact as subsidies affect only producers
Fall in food prices due to lower demand
questions.explanation: Lower subsidies raise farmers' cost, which is passed on, pushing food prices up.

questions.question: If the RBI's policy rate is unchanged but the government raises indirect taxes on petroleum products, the expected effect on CPI inflation will be:
options: Rise in headline CPI, little change in core CPI
Rise in both headline and core CPI equally
Fall in headline CPI, rise in core CPI
questions.explanation: Petroleum is a major weight in headline CPI but excluded from core CPI.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains the term 'inflation tax' in the Indian context?
options: Loss of real value of cash holdings due to price rise
questions.explanation: Inflation erodes purchasing power of money, acting like a tax on holders of cash.

questions.question: A rise in the fiscal deficit financed by market borrowing tends to:
options: Increase inflationary pressure via higher aggregate demand
questions.explanation: Borrowing raises demand and can be inflationary if output gaps are closed.

questions.question: Which of the following indicators is most reliable for assessing underlying inflation trends in India?
options: Producer Price Index (PPI) for manufactured goods
questions.explanation: Core CPI removes volatile food and fuel, reflecting underlying price pressures.

questions.question: If India's trade deficit widens because of higher import bills for oil, the likely impact on the RBI's monetary stance, assuming inflation is above target, will be:
options: Tighten policy to counter imported inflation
Loosen policy to support the current account
Maintain policy unchanged and rely on exchange market intervention
questions.explanation: Imported inflation pressures the RBI to tighten monetary policy to meet its inflation target.

questions.question: Which of the following combinations correctly describes the impact of a depreciating Indian rupee on inflation and the current account, assuming no change in monetary policy stance?
options: Higher import‑price inflation and widening current‑account deficit
Lower import‑price inflation and narrowing current‑account deficit
Higher import‑price inflation but improvement in current‑account due to higher export earnings
No effect on inflation and current‑account balance
questions.explanation: Depreciation raises import prices, pushing CPI up and widens the current‑account deficit unless export volumes rise sharply.

questions.question: The RBI conducts a sterilised intervention by selling government securities after buying foreign exchange. What is the primary macro‑economic objective of this operation?
options: To increase the money supply and boost growth
To prevent a rise in domestic interest rates caused by foreign‑exchange inflows
To raise the exchange rate by absorbing excess rupees
To reduce fiscal deficit through market operations
questions.explanation: Sterilisation offsets the liquidity impact of foreign‑exchange purchases, keeping interest rates stable.

questions.question: During a period of high food inflation, which fiscal measure is most likely to contain overall CPI without worsening the fiscal deficit significantly?
options: Increasing excise duty on petroleum products
Introducing a temporary subsidy on pulses
Raising the GST rate on essential food items
Providing targeted cash transfers to low‑income households
questions.explanation: Targeted cash transfers protect consumption while avoiding broad price hikes or large fiscal outlays.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the relationship between the fiscal deficit and inflation in India is most accurate?
options: A higher fiscal deficit always leads to higher inflation due to demand‑pull effects.
A rising fiscal deficit can be inflationary only if financed by monetising the deficit.
Fiscal deficit has no impact on inflation because monetary policy is independent.
Fiscal deficit influences inflation only through its effect on exchange rates.
questions.explanation: Inflationary pressure arises when the deficit is financed by creating money, not merely by its size.

questions.question: If the RBI raises the repo rate while simultaneously the government reduces GST on essential commodities, the net effect on headline inflation is likely to be:
options: Strongly inflationary because fiscal easing dominates monetary tightening
Neutral, as the two measures offset each other perfectly
Temporarily inflationary, followed by a decline as monetary tightening takes hold
Uncertain, depends on the relative magnitude of the rate hike and GST cut
questions.explanation: The outcome hinges on the size of each policy move; both affect demand and prices differently.

questions.question: Which component of the Balance of Payments is most directly affected by a rise in domestic inflation relative to trading partners?
questions.explanation: Higher domestic inflation makes exports less competitive, reducing the trade balance.

questions.question: The 'core inflation' metric used by the RBI excludes which of the following items?
questions.explanation: Core inflation removes volatile food and fuel components to gauge underlying price trends.

questions.question: Which of the following agricultural reforms is most likely to have a dampening effect on food price inflation in the long run?
options: Increasing minimum support price (MSP) for all crops
Allowing private warehousing and contract farming
Imposing export bans on cereals during a shortage
Providing direct cash subsidies to farmers for fertilizer
questions.explanation: Private warehousing and contract farming improve market efficiency and supply response, reducing price volatility.

questions.question: In the context of India’s inflation targeting framework, which statement best captures the role of the Monetary Policy Committee (MPC)?
options: It sets the CPI target and directly controls fiscal deficit.
It decides the policy repo rate to achieve the 4 ± 2 % CPI target.
It manages foreign‑exchange interventions to stabilise the rupee.
It allocates government borrowing to finance the fiscal deficit.
questions.explanation: The MPC’s primary function is to set the policy repo rate to meet the inflation target.

questions.question: If India experiences a supply‑side shock that raises the price of wheat, which of the following policy responses is most likely to contain the spill‑over to overall CPI without harming agricultural growth?
options: Tightening monetary policy by raising the repo rate
Increasing import duties on wheat to protect domestic farmers
Providing a temporary price ceiling on wheat in the open market
Expanding food‑grain procurement and releasing stocks from buffer reserves
questions.explanation: Releasing buffer stocks adds supply, curbing price rise while preserving farm incomes.

questions.question: Which of the following is a direct consequence of a sustained decline in the value of the Indian rupee against major currencies, assuming no change in domestic monetary policy?
options: Higher import‑derived inflation due to increased cost of foreign‑priced goods
Lower inflation because domestic production becomes cheaper for exporters
A rise in the real effective exchange rate, improving current account balance
A decrease in the money supply through automatic sterilisation by the RBI
questions.explanation: A weaker rupee raises the domestic price of imported goods, pushing CPI up; it can improve export competitiveness but not directly reduce inflation.

questions.question: When the RBI undertakes a sterilised intervention in the foreign‑exchange market by buying foreign currency and simultaneously selling an equivalent amount of domestic bonds, what is the primary effect on the short‑term interest rate?
options: It increases, as the money supply is reduced
It decreases, as the money supply is increased
It remains unchanged, because the intervention is sterilised
It fluctuates unpredictably due to market expectations
questions.explanation: Selling bonds absorbs liquidity, shrinking the money supply and pushing up short‑term rates, despite the currency purchase.

questions.question: Which fiscal indicator is most useful for gauging the potential inflationary pressure arising from current government spending patterns?
questions.explanation: A higher fiscal deficit signals increased borrowing or deficit financing, which can boost aggregate demand and inflation.

questions.question: How does the implementation of the 'Minimum Support Price' (MSP) for wheat affect food inflation in the short run?
